doc:
Pure-TS port of MPFR's mpfr_set_default_rounding_mode (mpfr/src/set_rnd.c L27-L32). The C function `void mpfr_set_default_rounding_mode(mpfr_rnd_t rnd)` stores `rnd` into the thread-global __gmpfr_default_rounding_mode IFF `rnd >= MPFR_RNDN && rnd < MPFR_RND_MAX`; otherwise it is a SILENT NO-OP (the global is left unchanged, no return code, no flag).

Integer codes (probed from this build's mpfr.h L100-L111): 0 RNDN, 1 RNDZ, 2 RNDU, 3 RNDD, 4 RNDA, 5 RNDF (faithful), with MPFR_RND_MAX == 6 and the retired MPFR_RNDNA == -1. So the C ACCEPTED set is the six codes 0..5 -- RNDF(5) IS accepted and stored (verified empirically). REJECTED are rnd < 0 (incl. RNDNA) and rnd >= 6, which leave the global at its prior value.

Wire form (immutable-API lift, INTEGER-coded): because the function's domain includes invalid codes (-1, 6, ...) that have no RoundingMode-string spelling, the port operates on the RAW integer code. It takes `(rnd: number, prior: number)` and returns a `number`: on an accepted rnd (0..5) it returns rnd; on a rejected rnd it returns `prior`. `prior` is threaded as an explicit input because the no-op branch's result depends on whatever rounding mode was live -- making it an input keeps every case deterministic and order-independent (the golden forces the global to `prior` before each call). The 0..4 <-> core.ts RoundingMode-string mapping lives in callers; RNDF(5) has no string spelling and is faithfully passed through at the integer level.

Class 'misc' (1000ms per-case budget). Tag minimums per Rule 7 satisfied: happy 20, edge 30, adversarial 12, fuzz 50, mined 5 (117 lines).
divergence_from_c:
Four divergences:

  1. Return value: C returns void (mutates the global); the port returns `number` -- the resulting default rounding-mode code (rnd if accepted, prior if rejected). Pure surface, no observable global mutation. The golden reads mpfr_get_default_rounding_mode() after the set to make the result observable.

  2. Explicit prior: the C no-op branch leaves the result equal to whatever mode was live. The port takes `prior` as a second input so the rejected-case output is deterministic; the golden forces the global to `prior` (always a valid code) immediately before the call under test.

  3. Integer wire (not RoundingMode string): the domain includes codes outside the five core.ts RoundingMode strings (negative codes, >= 6, and RNDF=5 which has no string). To express the bounds check faithfully -- including the RNDA(4)/RNDF(5)/RND_MAX(6) accept boundary -- the wire and port use the raw integer code on both input and output rather than the RoundingMode string. This is the standard integer-marker technique for predicate/bounds ports.

  4. RNDF acceptance: C accepts RNDF(5) (5 < RND_MAX=6) and stores it, even though src/core.ts RoundingMode is only the five strings RNDN..RNDA. The port faithfully returns 5 for rnd==5 (does NOT reject it). Treating RNDF as unsupported-as-a-RoundingMode is deferred to callers that convert an integer code to a RoundingMode string; this low-level setter mirrors the C accept-range 0..5 exactly. A port that rejects 5 (assuming 'five modes only' applies to the C accept-range) would FAIL the RNDF golden cases -- the brutal harness surfaces that mistake.
refs: mpfr/src/set_rnd.c L27-L32 -- mpfr_set_default_rounding_mode C body (bounds check + store, else no-op).
mpfr/src/set_rnd.c L34-L39 -- mpfr_get_default_rounding_mode companion (reads the global).
/usr/include/mpfr.h L100-L111 -- mpfr_rnd_t enum: 0=RNDN..4=RNDA, 5=RNDF, RNDNA=-1; the '0=RNDN..4=RNDA' comment.
src/core.ts L137-L151 -- RoundingMode: the five strings RNDN/RNDZ/RNDU/RNDD/RNDA (no RNDF, no RNDNA).
mpfr/tests/tset_rnd-style loops over RNDN..RNDA plus reset-to-RNDN (mined pattern).
